items.q: What did Bowlby mean by monotropy?
items.correct: One primary attachment figure (usually the mother) is qualitatively different and most important.
items.misconception: A child can only ever form one attachment.
items.why: Students take 'mono' literally as 'only one'. Bowlby recognised multiple attachments but argued the primary one was uniquely important for the internal working model.

items.q: In Pavlov's experiment, what is the conditioned response?
items.correct: Salivation to the bell alone, after pairing with food.
items.misconception: Salivation to the food (which is automatic).
items.random_wrong: The dog ringing the bell itself.
items.why: Students confuse the unconditioned response (automatic salivation to food) with the conditioned response (learned salivation to bell). The conditioned response is the LEARNED one.

items.q: How are phobias MAINTAINED according to Mowrer's two-process model?
items.correct: Through operant conditioning - avoidance reduces anxiety (negative reinforcement).
items.misconception: Through classical conditioning continuing over time.
items.why: Classical conditioning ACQUIRES the phobia; operant conditioning MAINTAINS it. Students conflate these. Avoiding the feared object reduces anxiety, reinforcing the avoidance.

items.q: Whose 1932 study used War of the Ghosts to demonstrate schema effects?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Bartlett. “Loftus” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Flashbulb memories were first studied by:
items.why: Correct answer: Brown and Kulik. “Loftus and Palmer” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: In what year did Tversky and Kahneman publish their heuristics paper?
items.why: Exact answer: 1974 AD. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
